milestone: Draft Asset Architecture, Phase B: World Builder v2 rewiring
slice: Rewires every real upload/read call site in World Builder v2 to go through the Phase A foundation (js/assetStore.js), closing the gap that phase deliberately left open ("nothing calls put()/resolve() yet"). _fileInputUpload (the ONE shared primitive every real upload -- Identity Thumbnail/Hero, Experience Image/Graphics, generic Assets-screen slots -- already funnels through) now calls a new _storeUploadedAsset(), which routes the downscaled data URI through AssetStore.put() and hands the resulting durable vihu-asset:<surface>:<projectId>:<assetId> reference to whatever setter would have received the raw base64 before -- a single fix point instead of touching each call site's own callback body. All ~14 identified read call sites (Welcome-screen project cards, Cloud World cards, the Overview identity card, the representative-artwork/Scene-Layer-image canvas caches, Scene sidebar rows, the Experience Library tab, Experience cards, generic Asset slot rows, World Card art) now resolve through a new shared _resolveAssetRefToSrc() wrapper -- a legacy data: URI still resolves same-tick with zero behaviour change; a vihu-asset: reference resolves asynchronously (warm cache -> IndexedDB -> a signed Storage URL), matching the same placeholder-then-resolve pattern _repoOnlyCard already established. Two real, load-bearing Build-time gaps were found and fixed at their own root: projectCompiler.js's _toBlob() gained a new branch resolving a top-level project.files entry's vihu-asset: reference to real bytes BEFORE the existing data:/else branches (a Theme's own Identity Thumbnail/Hero/Assets-screen uploads); builder.js's externalizeSceneImage() -- which had its own, separate 'is this a data: URI?' check gating whether a Scene Layer's image ever reaches the compiled package's assets map -- was made async and taught to resolve a vihu-asset: reference the identical way, closing a genuine silent-data-loss path a Scene-authored image would otherwise have hit (Build would have compiled a real relPath reference with NO backing bytes in the assets map). _syncUniversalContent's own Experience-to-Scene-Layer image mirror (js/projectModel.js) needed zero changes -- confirmed via direct code read to already be a pure string copy, so it naturally carries the new small reference string instead of ever re-uploading the same bytes twice. _rasterizeGlyphToDataURL (the Decorations quick-picker's tiny ~160x160 single-glyph PNG) is a disclosed, deliberate exception -- left as a plain data: URI, since its bytes are a few KB at most (nowhere near the scale that ever threatened localStorage's quota) and its one call site is a synchronous, multi-step sequence a Promise-based put() would need real restructuring to fit into for no benefit at this size. Verified via a new 22-assertion Playwright suite driving the REAL World Builder v2 UI end to end (not internals): a real image uploaded through the Overview Thumbnail row is stored as a vihu-asset: reference (not embedded base64), resolves correctly in both the Overview preview and the separate Welcome-screen project card, and a real ProjectCompiler.runBuild() produces a compiled package whose thumbnail.png asset bytes are BYTE-IDENTICAL to the original upload; a genuine kill-tab-mid-upload scenario (a real second upload caught pending mid-flight via a deliberately delayed mocked Storage call, then a real page reload) confirms retryPending() resumes and completes it automatically on the next boot; the resulting real v2-authored Project (now containing a genuine vihu-asset: reference produced by this actual upload path, not a hand-seeded fixture) still opens correctly in World Builder v1 via that version's own Phase A read-only mitigation. Both goldenBuild.js suites (World Builder v1 and v2, 30/30 each) and 6 existing scratchpad regression scripts covering Sign-In/Versioned Cloud Sync/Cloud Worlds Classification/Delete/No-Cascade-Delete all pass unchanged. Cache-bust bumped to ?v=0102 on every tag since this changes worldBuilderApp.js/projectCompiler.js (loaded from this same index.html).
